ii 
 
ABSTRACT 
This project focuses on developing a comprehensive web application for managing and 
booking tour packages using ASP.NET Core and SQL Server. The application enables 
users to browse and book tours, while administrators can manage tour details, including 
adding new packages with multiple day-wise hotel accommodations and images. Emphasis 
is placed on creating a robust backend with efficient data handling and secure user

authentication. Extensive testing, including input validation and error handling, ensures the 
reliability and usability of the system. The project demonstrates the integration of frontend 
technologies with a powerful backend framework to deliver a seamless user experience for 
both customers and administrators.

vii 
 
 
LIST OF ABBREVIATIONS  
API: 
Application Programming Interface 
ASP.NET: 
 Active Server Pages .NET 
DB: 
Database 
HTTP: 
Hypertext Transfer Protocol 
SQL: 
Structured Query Language 
UI: 
User Interface 
UX: 
User Experience 
CRUD: 
Create, Read, Update, Delete 
MVC: 
Model-View-Controller 
JSON: 
JavaScript Object Notation 
AJAX: 
Asynchronous JavaScript and XML 
REST: 
Representational State Transfer

1 
 
CHAPTER ONE 
INTRODUCTION 
1.1 Introduction 
The Travel Agency System provides an efficient and user-friendly platform for travelers to 
browse, select, and book tours based on ratings and reviews. Users are required to register 
an account by providing personal details such as name, username, phone number, email, 
etc. After registering, users can log in to the system using their username and password.

Once logged in, users can access their dashboard, where they can browse available tours, 
view personal information, and manage their bookings. In the "Browse Tours" section, 
users can explore different tour packages, check detailed descriptions, and compare ratings 
and reviews before making a booking. Additionally, users can rate tours and provide 
reviews based on their experience, which helps improve future recommendations. A

recommendation system suggests tours to users based on their previous bookings, location, 
and ratings. In the "Personal Info" section, users can view and update their profile details. 
The admin oversees the management of tour listings, user accounts, and booking records. 
The admin can also add new tour packages, update existing ones, and remove outdated or 
unavailable packages to ensure smooth operation and a high-quality user experience. 
1.2 Problem Statement

1.2 Problem Statement 
Despite the rapid growth of the tourism industry, many travelers face challenges in 
accessing accurate and up-to-date travel information, especially in emerging and less 
documented destinations. Countries like Nepal, which boast rich cultural heritage and 
breathtaking natural landscapes, often struggle with fragmented tourism services, lack of 
Reliable travel resources, and limited digital accessibility. Tourists frequently encounter

difficulties in finding trustworthy accommodation options, understanding local attractions, 
and planning efficient itineraries.  
To address these challenges, our Travel Agency System aims to create a centralized 
platform that bridges the gap between travelers and destinations, offering comprehensive, 
reliable, and user-friendly travel solutions. By integrating real-time data, AI-driven the

2 
 
travel planning experience, support local tourism economies, and promote 
responsible and sustainable tourism practices globally. 
1.3 Objectives 
The main objective of this project is to provide information about the essential and suitable 
Services for the Users.  
• Offering detailed travel guides and destination insights.  
• Providing an easy-to-use platform for booking accommodations and activities.  
• Enhancing user experience with interactive maps and itinerary planning.

• Encouraging user engagement through reviews, ratings, and travel blogs. 
1.4 Scope and Limitation 
1.4.1    Scope 
Comprehensive Travel Information: The platform will provide detailed insights into 
destinations, attractions, accommodations, and local experiences.  
• User Reviews and Ratings: A community-driven review system will help travelers 
make informed decisions.  
• Integrated Booking System: Our Travel Agency System will allow users to book 
accommodations and transportation.

accommodations and transportation.   
• Interactive Features: Users can engage with dynamic maps 
1.4.2 Limitation 
• Booking and reservation services will rely on external providers, which may affect 
availability and pricing.  
• Users need an active internet connection to access the platform and its features.  
• Complexity and issues in the System.  
• Expensive.

3 
 
1.5 Development Methodology 
For the ExploreEase travel booking system, the Waterfall Model was chosen because the 
project had clear and fixed requirements from the start, such as tour package browsing, 
booking management, review submission, and booking extension features. These 
requirements were discussed and finalized before development began, making a sequential 
development process appropriate. The Waterfall Model allowed the work to be organized

into distinct phases—requirement analysis, system design, implementation, testing, 
deployment, and maintenance—ensuring that each stage was completed before moving on 
to the next. This structure made it easier to track progress, maintain proper documentation, 
and reduce risks, as there was little likelihood of significant changes during the 
development process. Since the scope was well-defined and the project size was

manageable, the Waterfall Model provided an efficient and predictable workflow for 
successful completion. 
Figure 1.1: Waterfall Model

4 
 
1.6 Report Organization 
This report document contains five chapters including this chapter. In chapter one, we 
stated introduction, objectives, problem statement and limitation and scope of the system 
we made. Chapter two defines and describe Background study and Overview of related 
existing systems, and their pros and cons. Chapter three presents the System Analysis and 
Design including Requirement Analysis and Feasibility Analysis. Chapter four presents the

Implementation, Testing and debugging are explained. In chapter five, Conclusion, 
Limitation and Future Enhancement are briefly explained.

5 
 
CHAPTER TWO 
BACKGROUND STUDY AND LITERATURE REVIEW 
2.1 Background Study 
With the rapid growth of internet usage and digital services, the travel and tourism industry 
has increasingly moved towards online platforms. Tourists today prefer the convenience of 
browsing and booking tour packages online instead of visiting physical offices or relying 
on travel agents. Many global platforms like TripAdvisor, Booking.com, and Expedia have

set the standard by providing users with the ability to view ratings, read reviews, and 
compare tour options before making a decision. 
However, many local tour operators still lack a dedicated, transparent system where users 
can easily explore and book tours based on real customer feedback. Often, users are forced 
to rely on word-of-mouth or social media posts to evaluate the quality of a tour, which may 
not always be accurate or trustworthy.

not always be accurate or trustworthy. 
An Online Tour Booking System with a built-in rating and review feature bridges this gap 
by offering a centralized platform that is accessible, efficient, and transparent. It not only 
empowers users to make informed decisions based on reviews but also helps tour providers 
reach a wider audience and build credibility through consistent service quality. 
This system aims to improve the overall travel experience by making tour selection more

reliable and convenient, reducing dependency on manual processes, and encouraging 
healthy competition among tour providers based on service ratings. 
2.2 Literature Review 
The tourism industry has experienced significant transformation with the rise of digital 
platforms that facilitate travel planning, booking, and information sharing. Research 
suggests that online tourism platforms such as Booking.com,  
[1] TripAdvisor, and Expedia have revolutionized how travelers access information and

make bookings. These platforms provide user-generated content, reviews, and real-time 
pricing, which significantly influence travel decisions [2]User-generated reviews and peer 
recommendations play a crucial role in shaping travelers' decisions, as highlighted by 
research conducted by Litvin et al. (2008). To ensure reliable information, Travel Agency

6 
 
System will integrate a robust review and rating system. Additionally, sustainable tourism 
practices are gaining prominence, with studies emphasizing the importance of digital 
platforms in promoting eco-friendly travel options. Research highlights the growing 
demand for features such as carbon footprint tracking and green travel recommendations 
.Travel Agency System will integrate sustainability oriented features to encourage

responsible tourism. [3]Despite the advantages of digital tourism platforms, they face 
challenges such as misinformation, a lack of content on lesser-known destinations, and 
security concerns .To address these limitations, Travel Agency System will focus on 
providing verified content, real-time updates, and secure transaction processing. By 
leveraging insights from existing studies and best practices in the tourism industry, this

project aims to create a more efficient and user-friendly travel experience for global 
tourists.

7 
 
CHAPTER THREE 
SYSTEM ANALYSIS AND DESING 
3.1 System Analysis 
3.1.1 Requirement Analysis 
The system has various requirements that need to be considered, including how it should 
be built, what features it should offer, and which functions it must support. To gather these 
requirements, both primary and secondary research methods were used. Primary data was 
collected by interacting with users of existing systems and noting the features and

experiences they value most. Secondary data was gathered through online research and by 
analyzing current tour booking platforms to identify common practices and user 
expectations. This combined approach helped in forming a well-rounded understanding of 
what the new system should include. 
Functional Requirement 
This subsection contains the functional requirements for Tourism system. Features from 
proposal are refined into use case diagram to best capture the functional requirement of the

system.   
• Allow users to create an account with basic details (name, email, and password) and 
optional information.   
• Display a list of with details such as name, price, description and images.  
• Allow users to post reviews and blogs. 
• Allow users to log in and log out securely 
• Allow users to book tour packages by selecting available dates and providing 
necessary details. 
• Provide users with the ability to view their booking history and details.

• Enable users to extend or modify existing bookings within allowed limits. 
• Allow users to upload images along with their reviews and blog posts.

8 
 
                    
                      
 
Figure 3.1: Use Case Diagram  
Non-Functional Requirements 
The non-functional requirements of Tourism system are as follows: 
• Security: This system will be secure and the user’s information won't be available 
for others for user’s privacy  
• Performance: This system will be designed for smooth performance with 
optimization and good response. 
• Usability: The interface will be user-friendly and intuitive, enabling users of all

technical backgrounds to navigate the system easily. The system will provide clear 
error messages and helpful guidance where necessary. 
• Scalability: The system will be designed to accommodate growth in the number of 
users, bookings, and tour packages without significant degradation in performance. 
3.1.2 Feasibility Study 
Technical Feasibility

9 
 
The development of Travel Agency System requires a thorough analysis of the technical 
resources and infrastructure necessary for its implementation. The feasibility study assesses 
whether the proposed system can be developed with the available technologies, tools, and 
expertise. 
Operational Feasibility 
The operational feasibility of the Travel Agency System project focuses on assessing how 
well the system will function in real-world conditions and how efficiently it can be

integrated into the tourism industry. This includes evaluating user adoption, maintenance, 
and overall usability 
Economic Feasibility 
This project is designed to utilize the skills we've acquired during this period, making it 
both feasible and cost-effective. We're leveraging freely available resources from the 
internet, minimizing the need for significant financial investment. 
Schedule Feasibility 
This project is designated to complete within 150 days, and it is successfully completed

within timeline. No time exceed than our time bounds. So, this system is feasible. The work 
done through the setup designed is represented in below Gantt chart. 
   Figure 3.2 :Gantt chart 
 
This Gantt chart illustrates a comprehensive 5-month software development project 
spanning from March to July, showcasing a well-structured approach to project 
management. The project begins with a month-long planning phase in March, where teams

establish project requirements, define scope, and create strategic roadmaps. 
Simultaneously, the analysis phase starts mid-March and extends through mid-April,

10 
 
allowing for thorough examination of system requirements and business needs while 
overlapping with planning activities. 
The design phase runs from mid-March to mid-May, providing ample time for creating 
system architecture, user interface designs, and technical specifications. The most intensive 
period occurs during implementation, which spans three months from mid-April to mid-
July, representing the core development work where actual coding takes place. Notably,

unit testing runs parallel to implementation during this same timeframe, demonstrating best 
practices where individual components are tested as they're developed rather than waiting 
until the end. 
Final testing is scheduled for July, focusing on comprehensive system integration, 
performance testing, and user acceptance testing once all development is complete. 
Throughout the entire project lifecycle, documentation activities run continuously from

mid-March to the end of July, ensuring that all processes, decisions, and technical details 
are properly recorded and maintained. This timeline reflects an efficient waterfall 
methodology with strategic overlaps that maximize resource utilization and maintain 
project momentum. 
3.1.3 High Level Design of System 
High-Level Design (HLD) of the ExploreEase tourism website defines the overall system 
architecture and main components. The system allows users to browse and book tour

packages, make payments, and submit reviews, while administrators can manage tours, 
users, and bookings. It follows a three-layer architecture: Presentation Layer (ASP.NET 
Core Razor/React), Business Logic Layer (services handling bookings, payments, and 
reviews), and Data Access Layer (SQL Server database). HLD ensures all main features, 
data flow, and interactions are clearly defined and aligned with project requirements before 
detailed design and implementation. 
3.1.4 Object Modelling

3.1.4 Object Modelling 
The Class Diagram serves as a vital tool for representing object modelling within the scope 
of this project. Here different classes: admin, user, complaint are used which have their 
different methods with them.

11 
 
 
Figure 3.3 : Object & Class Diagram 
This UML Class Diagram depicts a tour booking system centered around five 
interconnected classes. The User class manages user authentication and profile information 
through Register(), Login(), and Logout() methods, while maintaining relationships with 
other system components. Users can write Reviews for TourPackages, make Bookings, and 
author Blog posts. The TourPackage class contains essential package details like name,

price, description, and images, with a GetDetails() method for information retrieval. 
The system allows users to provide feedback through the Review class, which captures 
ratings, comments, and review dates via the PostReview() method. Bookings are managed 
through the Booking class, offering functionality to make, extend, or cancel reservations 
with specific dates and package associations. The relationships between classes show a

comprehensive system where users can interact with tour packages through multiple 
touchpoints - booking services, reviewing experiences, and sharing content through the 
Blog class - creating an integrated travel platform.

12 
 
3.1.5 Dynamic Modelling 
Dynamic Modelling within the project is effectively conveyed through the utilization of 
state and sequence diagram. Sequence diagram and State diagram is provided here for user 
as well as admin. 
Figure 3.4 : Sequence Diagram 
This UML Sequence Diagram illustrates three main workflows in a tour booking system 
using a layered architecture with User and Admin actors interacting through UI, Controller, 
Service, Repository, and Database components.

Service, Repository, and Database components. 
The first scenario shows a user booking process where the User selects a tour package, 
triggering a request flow from UI through Controller and Service to Repository, which 
saves the booking in the Database. Confirmation messages flow back through each layer to

13 
 
display booking confirmation to the user. The second workflow demonstrates admin 
authentication, where login credentials are validated by fetching admin data from the 
Database through the system layers, ultimately granting dashboard access upon successful 
verification.The final scenario depicts an admin adding a new tour package, where package 
data flows through the same layered architecture for validation and storage. The diagram

effectively demonstrates separation of concerns in a multi-tier system, where UI handles 
user interaction, Controller manages routing, Service contains business logic, Repository 
manages data access, and Database provides persistence, with each layer confirming 
successful operations back through the chain 
 
Figure 3.5 : State Diagram

14 
 
The process begins when the user opens the website and enters the SEARCHING state, 
where they browse available destinations. Once they choose a tour package, they move to 
the SELECTING state to view detailed information about it. If they decide to proceed, they 
click “Book Now,” transitioning to the BOOKING state, where they fill in the booking 
form. Upon submitting the form, the process moves to the PAYMENT state, where the user

makes the required payment. If the payment is successful, the booking enters the 
CONFIRMED state, meaning the reservation is secured. From here, the process can either 
progress to the COMPLETED state after the tour takes place or be moved to the 
CANCELLED state if the user or admin cancels. Additionally, a payment failure or user 
cancellation during the payment stage will also lead directly to the CANCELLED state, 
marking the booking as closed. 
3.1.6 Process Modelling

3.1.6 Process Modelling 
The admin activity diagram is a key process-modelling component in my project. Admin 
log in, perform action based on their permissions, and have the option to log out in Travel 
Agency System. The users activity diagram is a pivotal element in my project’s process 
modelling. User login, enabling them to view dashboard where make complaint, resolved 
complaint, pending complaint, in progress complaint and personal info can see 
Figure 3.6 : User Activity Diagram

15 
 
This UML Activity Diagram illustrates the sequential workflow of a user booking process 
in a tour management system. The process begins when a user logs into the system, 
followed by browsing available tour packages and selecting a specific package of interest. 
Once a package is chosen, the user proceeds to enter their booking details, which triggers 
a critical validation step represented by a decision diamond asking "Are booking details

valid?".If the booking details fail validation (no path), the system displays an error message 
and loops back to the booking details entry step, allowing the user to correct and resubmit 
their information. However, if the validation succeeds (yes path), the system saves the 
booking to the database and displays a booking confirmation to the user. The workflow 
concludes with the user logging out of the system, terminating at the final end node. This

diagram effectively demonstrates error handling through the validation loop, ensuring data 
integrity before committing bookings to the database, while maintaining a clear linear 
progression for successful booking scenarios. 
 
Figure 3.6 : Admin Activity Diagram 
This UML Activity Diagram illustrates the administrative workflow for managing tour 
packages in a tour booking system. The process begins when an admin logs into the system

and reaches a decision point to "Choose action," which branches into four possible 
operations through a decision diamond labeled "Operation?" The admin can select to Add

16 
 
a new tour package, Edit an existing tour package, Delete a tour package, or Logout from 
the system. 
Each operation leads to its corresponding activity: "Add new tour package," "Edit existing 
tour package," "Delete tour package," or "Admin logs out." The first three operations (Add, 
Edit, Delete) converge at a "Continue?" decision point, where the admin can choose to 
perform additional operations by selecting "Yes" (which loops back to the "Choose action"

step) or end the session by selecting "No" (which terminates the process). The Logout 
option bypasses this decision and leads directly to the end state. This design allows 
administrators to perform multiple tour management operations in a single session, 
providing flexibility to add, modify, or remove tour packages continuously until they 
decide to exit the system, ensuring efficient bulk management of tour inventory. 
3.2 System Design

3.2 System Design 
In this system, it becomes the blueprint for the system, mapping out components, modules 
and data to meet precise requirements. I am actively involved in system development which 
encompasses creating and modifying system, involving various methodologies, model and 
practices to bring them to life. 
The system architecture serves as a comprehensive framework that defines the structural

foundation, establishing clear relationships between different layers including presentation, 
business logic, data access, and persistence tiers. This blueprint incorporates design 
patterns such as Model-View-Controller (MVC) and Repository patterns to ensure 
separation of concerns and maintainability. The development process follows established 
software engineering principles, utilizing both traditional waterfall and agile methodologies

depending on project requirements and stakeholder needs. Throughout the development 
lifecycle, various modeling techniques are employed including UML diagrams such as 
class diagrams for object-oriented design, sequence diagrams for interaction flows, state 
diagrams for behavior modeling, and activity diagrams for process workflows. These visual 
representations facilitate clear communication among team members and stakeholders

while serving as documentation for future maintenance and enhancements. The 
implementation phase involves writing clean, maintainable code following industry best 
practices such as SOLID principles, proper error handling, and comprehensive testing 
strategies.

17 
 
3.2.1 Refinement of Classes and Object 
Refinement means breaking down the high-level classes into more detailed classes by 
identifying attributes, methods, and relationships clearly to better represent the system’s 
real-world entities and their behaviors. 
 
Figure 3.7 : Activity Diagram 
This image presents a comprehensive class diagram for a sophisticated tour booking and 
management system, meticulously outlining the system's architecture, components, and

their interrelationships. At its core, the system is built around several key classes that model 
the essential entities and functionalities required for a full-featured booking platform. The

18 
 
foundation begins with the User class, which serves as the base entity containing common 
attributes like a read-only user ID, name, unique email address, encrypted password, and 
optional additional information. This base class also includes a registration method, 
establishing the fundamental user management capability.The system then extends this 
foundation through specialized user roles, demonstrating a clear inheritance structure. The

Customer class inherits from User and adds specific functionality tailored to end-users, 
including the ability to maintain a history of bookings and reviews, book tours, extend 
existing bookings, and view their booking history. On the administrative side, the Admin 
class (implied by the "AddTo" section) extends User with management capabilities like 
tour management, booking oversight, content moderation, and user administration, along

with an admin-specific role attribute. This role-based design ensures proper separation of 
concerns and access control within the system.Central to the system's functionality are the 
core business objects. The TourPackage class models the tour offerings with attributes like 
package ID, name, description, price (with a minimum value constraint), images, and 
availability status, along with methods to retrieve details. The Booking class handles

reservation tracking with attributes including booking ID, dates, status (using the 
BookingStatus enum with values like CONFIRMED and CANCELLED), and a method to 
calculate totals. The Review class manages user feedback with content, ratings (constrained 
to 1-5), approval status, and timestamps, while the Blog class handles article content with 
titles, body text, images, publication dates, and featured status markers.

The diagram also introduces key interfaces that standardize behavior across components. 
The Authenticatable interface defines essential authentication methods (login and logout), 
while the ContentManageable interface establishes consistent content operations (posting, 
editing, and deleting). These interfaces promote a modular design where different 
components can implement these standard behaviors as needed. Throughout the

specification, data integrity constraints are prominently featured, including read-only 
fields, unique email enforcement, encrypted passwords, value range restrictions, and status 
enumerations. The use of generic collections (like List~Booking~) demonstrates proper 
handling of object relationships.

19 
 
3.2.2 Component Diagram 
The component diagram of the Travel Agency System illustrates the high-level structure of 
the system by showing how different functional modules interact with each other. The 
system is divided into several key components to ensure modularity and maintainability. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure 3.8: Component Diagram 
This UML Deployment Diagram illustrates the architectural layers and components of a

tour booking web application system. The top layer represents the Web Application 
containing five key modules: User Interface (built with Razor Pages/JS for frontend 
interaction), Authentication Module (handling user login and security), Booking Module

20 
 
(managing tour reservations), Review & Blog Module (for user content creation), and 
Admin Panel (providing administrative controls). 
All frontend modules connect to the Backend Services layer, which serves as the central 
communication hub between the presentation layer and business logic. The backend 
contains Controllers that handle HTTP requests and route them to appropriate services, 
followed by the Service Layer that implements business logic and validation rules. Below

this sits the Repository Layer, which abstracts data access operations and provides a clean 
interface between business logic and data storage. 
The architecture follows a layered approach where each tier has distinct responsibilities: 
the web application handles user interaction and presentation, backend services manage 
request processing and business rules, and the SQL Server Database at the bottom provides

persistent data storage. This separation of concerns ensures maintainability, scalability, and 
testability, while the repository pattern decouples the application from specific database 
implementations. The arrows indicate the flow of data and requests through the system 
layers, demonstrating how user interactions flow down through the architecture to the 
database and responses flow back up to the user interface. 
3.2.3 Interface design (UI/UX)

3.2.3 Interface design (UI/UX) 
The UI design for the ExploreEase tourism system was created using Figma, focusing on 
delivering a user-friendly, visually appealing, and responsive experience. The design aims 
to make navigation intuitive, ensuring that users can easily browse tours, manage bookings, 
and interact with the platform on both desktop and mobile devices. A clean layout, 
consistent color palette, and clear typography were used to enhance usability and modern 
appeal.

21 
 
 
 
Login Page: 
 
The login page provides users with a simple and clean interface to access their accounts. It 
includes fields for email and password, a “Remember Password” option, and quick links 
for password recovery, new user registration, and email confirmation. The prominent blue 
login button ensures easy access. 
Dashboard Page:

22 
 
 
This is an administrative dashboard UI. It features a header with navigation links, similar 
to the previous image. The main content is titled "Dashboard" and displays key metrics in 
four cards: "Total Users," "Total Orders," "Total Revenue," and "Total Tours," with 
corresponding values. Below these metrics, there's a section for "Recent Order / Bookings." 
This section shows a single recent entry with a "Booking Id," "User" and the "Date" of the

booking, along with an associated amount displayed in a blue button. 
User Management Page: 
 
This UI is an administrative dashboard for user management. The page displays a 
navigation bar with links to various administrative functions, including blogs, booking, and 
service management. The main content area is a table that lists active users, showing their 
email addresses and full names, and provides an option to delete a user. The overall design
is clean, with a clear focus on managing user accounts.

23 
 
Order Management Page: 
 
This UI displays an Order Management page. The content is organized into three sections: 
Ongoing Tours, Upcoming Tours, and Done Tours. Each section has a table with columns 
for UserName, Price, Start Date, End Date, Booking Date, Number of people, and Total 
Price. There are also action links to delete or view details for each entry. The tables are 
populated with example data for various users and tour statuses.

24 
 
Manage Services Page: 
 
This UI is a Tour Package management page that displays tour packages in a grid of cards. 
Each card features an image placeholder, tour destination, price, and a star rating. For 
administrative purposes, each card also includes controls, likely in the form of buttons or 
links, to update the price and delete the specific tour package.

25 
 
Landing page: 
 
The landing page of ExploreEase presents a clean, visually appealing layout with a 
prominent search bar over a background image for quick destination searches. It showcases 
a “Recommended for You” section for personalized suggestions and an “All Tour 
Packages” section to explore available options. Each package card displays the tour image, 
title, duration, rating, destination, and price, making it easy for users to compare and 
choose.

26 
 
3.2.4 Deployment Diagram 
 
Figure 3.9 : Deployment Diagram 
This UML Deployment Diagram shows a three-tier tour booking system with Client 
Machine (Web Browser/Mobile App) communicating via HTTP/HTTPS and REST API to 
a Web Server containing UI Layer and API Controllers. The Web Server connects to 
Business Logic services (Booking, Content, Auth) that perform CRUD, query, and 
verification operations on a Database Server with SQL Server storing Bookings, Tour

Packages, and User Data tables. 
3.3 Algorithm Details 
In our project, we use a content-based filtering recommendation/Location-based 
recommendation algorithm using Haversine distance and rating prioritization that suggests 
items based on the user's recent orders, geographical proximity, and customer ratings. By

27 
 
calculating the latitude and longitude of the user's latest order, the system identifies nearby 
options. It then prioritizes recommendations using star ratings to ensure quality, delivering 
personalized and relevant suggestions effectively. 
Recommendation Algorithm Explanation 
The system recommends tour packages to a user based on their most recent booking 
location and the distance to other tour packages, prioritizing nearby tours with higher

ratings. The algorithm works as follows: 
Fetch User Payment History: 
Retrieve all previous bookings for the user and identify the most recent booking. Let the  
Let the latest booked tour package ID be PLATEST, and the coordinates of that package be 
(latU,lonU). 
Filter Tour Packages: 
Exclude the last booked package from the list of all available tour packages to avoid 
recommending the same tour again. 
Distance Calculation:

Distance Calculation: 
For each remaining tour package with coordinates (latI,lonI ), calculate the great-circle 
distance from the last booked package using the Haversine formula: 
• Convert all Coordinates from degree to radians: 
rad =  deg × 𝜋
180 
• Use the  radians(rad) in place of degrees respectively. 
• Apply the formula 
∆𝑙𝑎𝑡= 𝑙𝑎𝑡𝐼−𝑙𝑎𝑡𝑈 
∆𝑙𝑜𝑛= 𝑙𝑜𝑛𝐼−𝑙𝑜𝑛𝑈 
𝑎= 𝑠𝑖𝑛2 (∆lat
2 ) + 𝑐𝑜𝑠(𝑙𝑎𝑡U). 𝑐𝑜𝑠(𝑙𝑎𝑡I). sin2 (∆lon
2 ) 
𝑐= 2. 𝑎𝑟𝑐𝑡𝑎𝑛2(√a , √1-a ) 
𝑑= 𝑅. 𝑐 
Where R=6371km is the Earth’s radius.

𝑑= 𝑅. 𝑐 
Where R=6371km is the Earth’s radius. 
Sorting and Ranking: 
• Sort the tour packages by distance (nearest first). 
• If multiple packages have the same distance, sort by rating (highest first).

28 
 
Top Recommendations: 
Select the top 5 tour packages as recommendations for the user. 
Illustrative Calculation Example 
We have: 
• Last Booked Package : (latU,lonU) = (27.7 , 85.3) 
• PackageA: (latA,lonA) = (27.8,85.4) 
• Package B : (latB,LonB) =(27.6,85.5) 
• Earth radius R=6371 km 
Convert degree to radians 
27.7𝑜  × 𝝅
180 =  0.48332 𝑟𝑎𝑑 
27.8𝑜  × 𝝅
180 =  0.48502 𝑟𝑎𝑑 
27.6𝑜  × 𝝅
180 =  0.48158 𝑟𝑎𝑑 
85.3𝑜  × 𝝅

85.5𝑜  × 𝝅

dB  =   6371 ⋅0.00356 
dB  =   22.66km 
Final result : 
• Distance to Package A: 14.84 km 
• Distance to Package B: 22.66 km 
The algorithm would rank Package A higher based on proximity.

CHAPTER FOUR 
IMPLEMENTATION AND TESTING 
4.1 Implementation 
In this project, the implementation phase involved turning the system’s designs, UML 
diagrams, and UI/UX prototypes into a fully functional tourism management platform. The 
development followed a structured, layered architecture to separate concerns and maintain 
code clarity. 
The core functionalities, including tour package management, recommendations, user

authentication, and content posting, were coded to match the planned requirements. The 
interface was built according to the Figma designs, ensuring a visually appealing and user-
friendly experience. 
Once the implementation was complete, the system underwent rigorous testing to detect 
and fix any defects early. This helped ensure that the final product was reliable, secure, and 
performed efficiently before deployment. 
4.1.1 Tool used

4.1.1 Tool used 
In our project, we use ASP.NET Core for backend development and SQL Server for 
database management. Visual Studio is used as the main IDE for coding and project 
management, while SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS) is used to manage and query 
the database.  
Language used 
• For front end: HTML, CSS, JavaScript 
• For backend: .NET Core 
• Database: SQL Server 
Project Technology Stack Explanation

Project Technology Stack Explanation 
Our project is developed using a modern and efficient technology stack to ensure robust 
functionality, maintainability, and ease of management. 
Backend Development: 
We utilize ASP.NET Core as the backend framework. ASP.NET Core is a cross-
platform, high-performance framework that enables us to build scalable and secure web 
applications. It provides powerful features such as dependency injection, middleware

31 
 
pipelines, and easy integration with various databases, which help streamline the 
development process and enhance application performance. 
Frontend Development: 
The frontend of the application is built using standard web technologies: 
HTML for structuring the content on the web pages, 
CSS for styling and layout to ensure an attractive and responsive user interface, 
JavaScript to add interactivity and dynamic behavior, making the user experience more 
engaging and responsive.

engaging and responsive. 
Database Management: 
For data storage and management, we use Microsoft SQL Server, a robust relational 
database management system known for its reliability, security features, and efficient 
query processing. SQL Server allows us to design complex schemas, enforce data 
integrity, and perform advanced queries to retrieve and manipulate data effectively. 
Development Tools: 
Visual Studio is the primary Integrated Development Environment (IDE) used for writing,

debugging, and managing the ASP.NET Core codebase. Visual Studio provides rich tooling 
support, including IntelliSense, debugging, and integrated source control, which 
significantly improves development productivity. 
SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS) is employed to manage the SQL Server 
databases. SSMS offers a user-friendly interface to create, modify, and query the database, 
as well as perform tasks like backup, restore, and performance tuning.

4.1.2. Implementation Details of Modules (Admin and User) 
1. User Module 
The User module is responsible for managing all user-related functionalities, primarily 
targeted at travelers who use the system to explore and book tour packages. This module 
ensures a smooth and secure experience for regular users by providing the following 
features: 
User Login: 
This method(OnPostAsync()) handles the login process asynchronously. It first checks if

33 
 
 
User Registration: 
This asynchronous method handles user registration. It first validates the input, then creates 
a new user with the provided details, including full name and email. After successfully 
creating the user account with a password, it assigns a default "User" role, automatically 
confirms the user's email, and signs them in. If any errors occur during registration, they 
are added to the model state to inform the user.

34 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Profile Management: 
This method (OnPostAsync()) handles password reset requests. It first checks if the 
submitted email is valid and corresponds to a confirmed user account. If so, it generates a 
secure password reset token, encodes it, and creates a reset link. This link is sent to the 
user's email. For security, it does not reveal whether the email exists or is confirmed. 
Finally, it redirects to a confirmation page. 
 
 
            // Sign in the user

35 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Tour Booking: 
The controller’s Payment method receives the payment form data from a logged-in user 
and calls the service to process it. The service method InsertIntoPayment reads and 
parses the form values, calculates the total price, and creates a payment record with all 
necessary details like dates, number of people, price, and user info. It then saves this record 
to the database via the repository method InsertIntoDB. Based on whether the save is

successful, the controller returns a success or error view. If any error occurs during 
processing or saving, the method returns failure.

36 
 
 
 
 
 
 
View Bookings (My Orders): 
The UserOrder method in the controller retrieves the logged-in user’s email and fetches 
their booking orders via the service layer, then displays the results in a view. The ExtendDate 
method accepts a request to extend a booking’s end date by first retrieving the number of 
days from the tour package, then calling the service to update the booking accordingly. The

service layer acts as a bridge, calling repository methods to get user orders and to extend 
the booking date. In the repository, the orders are fetched from the database by matching 
the user’s email, and the booking extension updates the end date by adding the specified 
number of days, marks the booking as extended, and saves the changes. The methods return 
success or failure based on whether the database operations complete without errors.

37 
 
 
Review Submission: 
The controller method SubmitReview allows a logged-in user to submit a review for a tour 
package. It first verifies the user’s identity, then calls the service layer to insert the review 
details into the database. After successfully saving the review, it updates the overall rating 
of the related tour package and marks the associated payment as reviewed. The service 
layer handles the business logic and calls the repository, which creates a new review record

38 
 
 
Blog posting: 
This repository method PostBlog adds a new blog post, which includes the blog’s image 
and text, to the database asynchronously. It inserts the provided blog model—containing 
the image data and blog content—into the blog table and saves the changes. The method 
returns true if the operation succeeds by affecting one or more records; otherwise, it returns 
false.

2. Admin Module 
The Admin module is designed to give administrative personnel full control over the travel 
agency system. It allows the admin to manage the platform’s content, monitor user activity, 
and maintain operational efficiency. 
Dashboard Overview: 
This method fetches various summary data to display on the dashboard. It retrieves the total 
number of users from the user manager, and gets the total orders, total revenue, and total

tours from the order services. Additionally, it fetches recent orders, the latest users based 
on their IDs, and recent reviews using respective service methods. All this data is then 
passed to the view using ViewBag to be displayed to the user.

40 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Tour Package Addition: 
This service method processes the form data submitted to create a new tour package. It 
extracts key details such as the tour name, description, number of days, price, destination, 
and geographic coordinates. It also handles uploading and saving the main destination 
image. For each day of the tour, it collects hotel details including name, description, and

location, along with multiple images for each hotel, saving those images appropriately. 
After gathering all this information, the method calls the repository to insert the tour 
package along with its related hotels and images into the database. The method returns a 
success result if everything is saved correctly or returns an error message if any exception 
occurs during the process

4.2. Testing  
Testing is evaluation of the software against requirements gathered from users and system 
specifications. Testing identifies important defects, flaws, or an error in the application 
code that must be fixed. It also assesses the features of a system. Testing assesses the quality 
of the product. The Vehicle Renting System was tested both individually and all together. 
4.2.1. Test Cases for Unit  Testing

4.2.1. Test Cases for Unit  Testing  
Unit testing is the process of testing individual components or functions of a software 
application in isolation to ensure they work correctly. It focuses on the smallest parts of the 
code, like methods or classes. 
What do we do? 
• Test a single function or method independently. 
• Use test inputs and check if the output or behavior is as expected. 
• Mock or simulate dependencies to isolate the unit being tested.

• Catch bugs early in the development cycle. 
• Automate tests so they can run frequently.

4.2.2. Test Cases for System Testing  
System testing is the process of testing the complete and integrated software system to 
verify that it meets all requirements. It evaluates the system’s behavior as a whole 
What do we do? 
• Test the entire application in an environment similar to production.

47 
 
• Verify end-to-end workflows and integration between components. 
• Check functional requirements, performance, security, and usability. 
• Validate how the system handles real user scenarios and data. 
• Identify any issues before release.

CHAPTER FIVE 
 CONCLUSION AND FUTURE RECOMMENDATIONS 
5.1 Lesson Learnt 
This project has provided valuable experience in developing a complete web-based travel 
agency system with features such as tour package management, user booking, location-
based recommendations, and online payment integration. It enhanced understanding of 
both client-side (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) and server-side (ASP.NET Core, C#) 
development. .Through this project, knowledge was gained in database design, data

retrieval using LINQ, and layered architecture involving repositories, services, and 
controllers. It also improved familiarity with AJAX-based form submissions, payment 
gateway integration, and user-centric design principles. In addition, this project encouraged 
better team collaboration, time management, and the ability to apply theoretical knowledge 
from software engineering and database management courses in a real-world application.

Overall, it significantly contributed to skills in web development, problem solving, and 
project execution  
5.2 Conclusion 
The project successfully delivers a functional and user-friendly tour and travel booking 
system. It offers an attractive interface and smooth navigation for both users and admins. 
The system allows users to explore travel packages, make bookings, submit reviews, and 
receive personalized tour recommendations based on previous bookings.This travel agency

system fulfills its defined objectives, such as improving the booking experience, enhancing 
accessibility to travel information, and reducing the manual workload of tour operators. It 
also addresses limitations found in traditional systems by offering real-time booking, geo-
location-based suggestions, and better user engagement. 
5.3 Future Recommendations 
The following improvements are recommended for future versions of the travel agency 
system:

51 
 
• 
Enhance User Interface by adding more interactive elements, animations, and 
responsive layouts for mobile devices. 
• 
Integrate Advanced Filters to allow users to search packages by location, price 
range, duration, or theme. 
• 
Add Multi-language Support to make the platform more accessible to 
international users. 
• 
Improve Security by adding two-factor authentication, CAPTCHA, and secure 
payment handling.
